ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific term for Punjabi Channa Masala; closest defensible match is 'chickpea dish' (FOODON_03311737). Exact term absent.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific composite curry code for Punjabi Channa Masala. 'Chickpeas, cooked' (A0CKP) is the closest defensible base-ingredient anchor. The masala gravy components are not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT concept identified for Punjabi Channa Masala as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT food hierarchy does not extend to this level of regional specificity.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Punjabi Channa Masala is approximately 300 g, representing one main-course portion as commonly served in North Indian restaurants in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Chana Masala (Chickpea Curry) — ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods, adapted composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) provides values for cooked chickpeas (kabuli chana, boiled) and for chickpea-based curry preparations. The per-100g values below are adapted from the ICMR-NIN chickpea curry composite (chickpeas cooked with onion-tomato-spice gravy and oil), cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for 'Chickpeas, mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt' (FDC ID 173757) for micronutrient detail. The composite reflects a typical Punjabi Channa Masala with approximately 55% chickpeas, 30% gravy (onion, tomato, spices), and 15% oil contribution by cooked weight.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — chickpeas, boiled (Cicer arietinum)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: The University of Sydney GI Database and the 2008 International GI Tables (Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008) report a GI of approximately 28 (±9) for boiled chickpeas (kabuli chana) tested against glucose reference. Punjabi Channa Masala is a chickpea-dominant dish (approximately 55% chickpeas by cooked weight); the addition of onion-tomato gravy and spices does not materially raise the GI of the chickpea base. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (28 × 52.5 g carbohydrate in 300 g serving) / 100 = 14.7. This is classified as a medium GL per serving despite low GI, due to the large restaurant portion size.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from added salt and spice blends
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of Punjabi Channa Masala contributes approximately 960 mg sodium, representing 42–48% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to 150 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (GI 28) and high dietary fibre (4.8 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Punjabi Channa Masala has a low GI of 28 driven by the resistant starch and soluble fibre in chickpeas, which attenuates postprandial glucose response; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be mindful of the medium glycemic load (GL ~14.7) from a full 300 g restaurant portion and pair the dish with non-starchy vegetables rather than rice or naan to manage total carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High soluble fibre, plant protein, and low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The soluble fibre (approximately 14.4 g per 300 g serving) and plant-based protein from chickpeas support LDL cholesterol reduction; the dish is cholesterol-free and low in saturated fat (0.7 g per 100 g), making it a cardioprotective choice when prepared with minimal added oil and salt.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Punjabi Channa Masala is a classic North Indian legume curry originating from the Punjab region of the Indian subcontinent. It is prepared by slow-cooking kabuli chana (white chickpeas) in a richly spiced onion-tomato gravy that is built by sautéing finely chopped onions until golden, then adding ginger-garlic paste, green chillies, tomatoes, and a blend of whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, garam masala, and amchur (dried mango powder), which gives the dish its characteristic tangy depth. The chickpeas are pre-soaked overnight and pressure-cooked or boiled until tender before being simmered in the gravy to absorb the spice flavours. The finished dish has a thick, dark, aromatic sauce with a robust, earthy, mildly tangy flavour profile and a hearty, slightly firm chickpea texture. It is garnished with fresh coriander leaves and sometimes thin ginger julienne. Nutritionally, Punjabi Channa Masala is a high-fibre, plant-protein-rich dish with approximately 7.2 g protein and 4.8 g dietary fibre per 100 g, making it one of the most nutritionally dense vegetarian curries in Indian cuisine. Its low glycemic index of 28 makes it suitable for blood glucose management. The dish is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. In Singapore, it is commonly served as a main course in North Indian restaurants alongside naan, roti, or basmati rice, and is a staple on the menus of restaurants such as Curry Village, Mr Tandoor, Bawarchi, Bombay Grill, and Aryana. Clinically, it is a favourable choice for cardiovascular health due to its soluble fibre content and absence of cholesterol, though its sodium content from restaurant preparation warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Punjabi Channa Masala as a prepared restaurant dish exists in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked chickpea curry data and USDA chickpea micronutrient profiles, with a composite estimate reflecting typical restaurant-style preparation. GI is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for boiled chickpeas as an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the chickpea base is well-characterised in approved sources and the adaptation methodology is transparent.
notes: Nutrient values are per-100g estimates adapted from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017) chickpea curry composite and USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 173757) for micronutrient detail. Sodium estimate (320 mg/100g) reflects typical restaurant preparation with added salt; home-prepared versions may be significantly lower. Cholesterol is 0.0 mg as this is a plant-based dish with no animal-derived ingredients — confirmed zero per ICMR-NIN and USDA chickpea data. Vitamin B12 is 0.0 as this is a vegan dish with no animal-derived ingredients — naturally zero. Vitamin D is 0.0 — naturally zero for this plant-based preparation with no fortification. Trans fat is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero in plant-oil-based Indian curry preparations per ICMR-NIN. FoodOn match is closest_parent (chickpea dish) rather than exact; no specific Punjabi Channa Masala term exists in FoodOn as of April 2026. FoodEx2 code A0CKP (chickpeas, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor. SNOMED CT does not have a specific concept for this dish. field survey source data preserved for restaurant provenance context only; not used as nutritional evidence. Price range SGD 15.90–16.90 from field survey data (5 restaurants). Follow-up: if ICMR-NIN publishes updated composite values for chickpea-based curries, re-evaluate per_100g values.
